title: What is the Mission of the Church, Part 5: Understanding God's Redemptive Role
speaker: Elder Nick Anderson
series: What is the Mission of the Church
source_files: What is the Mission of the Church 5 - Understanding God's Redemptive Role - Elder Nick Anderson.txt
outline: Introduction: God's redemptive role in mission
The tension between sovereignty and human responsibility
God's plan of redemption from eternity
How understanding God's role empowers mission
Conclusion: Confidence in mission grounded in God's sovereignty
summary: Elder Nick Anderson continues the mission series in part 5, examining God's redemptive role in mission. He addresses the relationship between God's sovereign election and the church's evangelistic responsibility.
transcript:
## Introduction

We are in chapter five of *What Is the Mission of the Church?* The chapter is titled "Kings and Kingdoms: Understanding God's Redemptive Role."

I could relate to what the author said at the beginning — he didn't really learn much about the kingdom of God growing up in the church. I didn't either. I heard the term, but didn't really know what it meant. But it is extremely important and it is throughout the Bible. It is the first thing that Jesus preaches about:

> *"From that time Jesus began to preach and to say, 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.'"* (Matthew 4:17)

My goal is to go through what the authors teach, but also to tie it into the rest of the book — because I think that is one thing the authors could have done better in chapter five. So we have good teaching about the kingdom of God, but how does it affect the way we think about the mission of the church? I hope to tie those things together.

## Defining the Kingdom of God

The terms "kingdom of God" and "kingdom of heaven" are uniquely New Testament terms, though the *concept* of God's kingdom is throughout the Old Testament. God creates man and gives him a law. Man falls. God establishes a people for himself, calls Abraham, gives a fuller law to the Hebrew people, and eventually establishes a king over them. Throughout the Old Testament there is this concept: God is the King, and he will reign over his people. That theme comes to a more full fulfillment in the New Testament — and will be ultimately fulfilled when Christ returns.

It was a major theme of Jesus's preaching and the apostles' teaching. Paul in Acts 28:31 was in Rome "preaching the kingdom of God and teaching the things which concern the Lord Jesus Christ with all confidence."

In chapter one, the authors made this important point: "We want the church to remember that there is something worse than death, and something better than human flourishing. If we hope only for renewed cities and restored bodies in this life, we are of all people most to be pitied."

When missional thinking becomes all about tackling social problems and injustices — without making disciples, without preaching the gospel, without bringing people into the kingdom of God — it actually ceases to be Christian thinking. At that point, if making cities more just is your church's goal, you are running a charity organization. You are not running a Christian church.

## Four Truths About the Kingdom of God

### 1. The Kingdom of God Is the Redemptive Rule of God Over His People

The kingdom of God is not a geographical rule — there are no more physical borders as in the Old Testament. In the New Testament, Christ's rule is over his people; it is dynamic and relational. We are foreigners in our earthly existence:

> *"These all died in faith, not having received the promises, but having seen them afar off were assured of them, embraced them and confessed that they were strangers and pilgrims on the earth."* (Hebrews 11:13)

> *"Beloved, I beg you as sojourners and pilgrims, abstain from fleshly lusts which war against the soul."* (1 Peter 2:11)

We are not called citizens of earth. We are sojourners and pilgrims. We should not feel at home on earth.

The rule of God is specifically over his *redeemed* people — not everyone. Note the passages that make this clear:

- > *"Let the little children come to Me, and do not forbid them; for of such is the kingdom of God."* (Mark 10:14)
- > *"How hard it is for those who trust in riches to enter the kingdom of God! It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich man to enter the kingdom of God."* (Mark 10:24–25)
- > *"There will be weeping and gnashing of teeth when you see Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob and all the prophets in the kingdom of God, and yourselves thrust out."* (Luke 13:28)
- > *"Do you not know that the unrighteous will not inherit the kingdom of God?"* (1 Corinthians 6:9)
- > *"He has delivered us from the power of darkness and conveyed us into the kingdom of the Son of His love."* (Colossians 1:13)

None of these verses have anything to do with social programs, soup kitchens, human trafficking, or equity. The only way God's kingdom is extended is when another soul is redeemed by Christ's saving work. God's kingdom does not include those who are unsaved.

Those of us who are redeemed can and should bear witness to the kingdom of God and live like citizens of it. We should preach the kingdom of God as Christ and the apostles did. But social programs do not bring people into the kingdom of God who are not in it.

The mission of the church as an institution is to bring people into the kingdom of God by the proclamation of the gospel and making disciples. Social renewal is not the institutional mission of the church. It may be a mission for individual Christians — and that is a good thing. But if you are serving at the Union Gospel Mission or fighting human trafficking and you are not proclaiming the gospel at the same time, you are doing charity work. You are leaving something out that ought to be there.

As the book says earlier: if we fail to relieve eternal suffering while simultaneously relieving temporal suffering, all we are doing is making the road to hell more comfortable.

### 2. The Kingdom of God Is the Reign of the Messiah Jesus

> *"He has delivered us from the power of darkness and conveyed us into the kingdom of the Son of His love."* (Colossians 1:13)

It is Christ's kingdom:

> *"My kingdom is not of this world."* (John 18:36)

And the thief on the cross understood this:

> *"Lord, remember me when You come into Your kingdom."* (Luke 23:42)

Jesus did not correct him. He simply said, "Today you will be with Me in Paradise."

There is only one mediator of this kingdom:

> *"For there is one God and one Mediator between God and men, the Man Christ Jesus."* (1 Timothy 2:5)

> *"Nor is there salvation in any other, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved."* (Acts 4:12)

Since Christ reigns over his people and is the only mediator, we should ask: what does Christ want us to do? He gave us a specific task in the Great Commission. And I will say plainly: it is much easier to be a blessing to people than to share the gospel with your sister who refuses to consider coming to church. It is much easier to help the poor or address affordable housing than to clearly explain the gospel to a neighbor. I think that is partly why the "just be a blessing" teaching is so attractive — it is easier. But if we ignore the Great Commission — the very last thing Christ told us to do before he ascended — we are not fulfilling the mission of the church.

### 3. The Kingdom of God Involves the Age to Come Breaking Into the Present Age

The Bible gives us pictures of what the kingdom will ultimately be like. Isaiah 65 tells us of joy, happiness, no tears, no death, no sickness — perfect peace and security. Revelation echoes the same, describing the new Jerusalem where God and his people dwell together perfectly forever.

But we are not there yet. The kingdom of God is here *in part*. This overlap is what theologians call "inaugurated eschatology" — the kingdom has broken into the present age but is not yet fully realized.

> *"And it shall come to pass afterward that I will pour out My Spirit on all flesh..."* (Joel 2:28–29)

We live in this overlap: redeemed people who still struggle against sin; who enjoy the presence of the Spirit yet still grieve him at times; raised with Christ yet still awaiting the final resurrection.

In this overlap, we ought to pursue the mission of the church — proclaiming the gospel and making disciples — knowing that on this side of heaven we will never solve all the problems we see around us. Dreams of a utopian society are foolhardy. But because the age to come has invaded the present age, we can go forth boldly, proclaiming the gospel and making disciples. Not because we think we can end hunger and poverty, but because we know that poverty and hunger are not the greatest problem people face.

As the book notes: Christ never went into a city with the stated purpose of healing the sick or feeding the hungry. He did those things — but the message was *"Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand."* He always went to relieve people's eternal condition.

### 4. The Kingdom of God Is Manifested in the Present Age in the Church

In this present age, the kingdom is manifested in the church — and only in the church.

> *"On this rock I will build My church."* (Matthew 16:18)

> *"Having been built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Jesus Christ Himself being the chief cornerstone."* (Ephesians 2:20)

> *"...to the intent that now the manifold wisdom of God might be made known by the church to the principalities and powers in the heavenly places."* (Ephesians 3:10)

The proclamation of the gospel, which happens in the church, is how the kingdom of God is made known to the world. Social programs do not proclaim the kingdom of God in and of themselves. There are many good things we can and should do. But the mission of the church is to proclaim the gospel and make disciples.

## When Will the Kingdom Be Finally Established?

Only when Christ returns. Matthew 24 and Revelation 19–20 make this clear.

Can social renewal happen? Yes — but only as a byproduct of the gospel being proclaimed to all nations. What happens when the gospel reaches one person? Their heart is changed. Their desires are changed. They turn from doing evil. What happens when the gospel reaches a nation? The hearts of the people are changed, which changes society. Everywhere the gospel has gone, literacy rates go up. The value of human life is elevated. The place of women in society is elevated. That is the result of the gospel taking hold in people's hearts.

The kingdom of God certainly grows. As more souls are added to it, it becomes a greater influence. Jesus's parable of the mustard seed makes exactly this point. We are employed in that work — you and I, as the church, are employed in the proclamation of the gospel and the making of disciples. Every redeemed person in this room has a role to play in each other's lives. I need you; you need me to grow and become better disciples and proclaim the gospel more clearly.

We do not make the kingdom grow — it is the work of the Spirit of God, just as we do not sanctify ourselves, though we are called to work out our salvation with fear and trembling. There is work to be done. It is our mission. And we must go do it with boldness, knowing that Christ does reign over the kingdom of God and he will succeed.
